sections.title: Chairman's Chat
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports

Not much committee business this month so you’re going to get another download of my Army Flying Career.  Previous chapters, for insomniacs:

Chapter 1 - Sycamore Seeds

Chapter 2 - Parachuting

Chapter 3 - Reserve Deployment

Chapter 4 - Alcohol Makes Friends

Chapter 5 – Happy Hookers

Chapter 6 – Aptitude Tests

Flying Grading

Having passed the aptitude tests and bluffed my way through the interview, there was one more significant hurdle to overcome before being loaded on the Army Pilot Course: thirteen hours of flying in a fixed wing aeroplane with an experienced instructor watching you like a hawk and assessing your ability to assimilate information and follow instructions.  It was made very clear to us that whilst, on average, only 10% of candidates fail the Pilot’s Course; only 10% of candidates pass grading.  No pressure there then!

And so it was that on 27 January 1992, I found myself kitted out with an olive-green flying suit, an aircrew helmet complete with integral dark visor, and a pair of ‘oh so soft’ white leather flying gloves.

The instructor showed us how to walk around the aircraft checking that everything was serviceable.  As I recall he only did this once, trusting us to do it on all subsequent flights.  I think that this gesture was more a sign of confidence in the aircraft than the student.  The aircraft in question was a Chipmunk T10, built in the 1950s and long since consigned to a museum.  Something from a previous generation: rudimentary and reliable.  Starting required the assistance of a ground crewman who would prime the engine, but he wasn’t going to put his hands anywhere near the propeller until the pilot (me!) showed his hands outside the cockpit (remember the white gloves) and shouted out a phrase that I will take to my grave, ‘fuel on, brakes on, throttle closed, switches off’.  Once primed the final impetus to encourage the engine into life was a shotgun cartridge (with the shot removed), initiated by the pilot pulling a cable.  I kid you not.

Taxying wasn’t easy.  The Chipmunk is a ‘tail-dragger’ (two main wheels at the front and one smaller one at the back).  This means that the tail is much lower than the propeller.  Until you reach flying speed, when the tail lifts off the ground, the pilot’s view is obstructed by the nose (say 11 o’clock to 1 o’clock) so he can’t see where he is going.  The solution is to use the rudder to swerve from side to side constantly checking for potential obstacles.  The instructor, sitting behind you, could see even less.

As I recall the first flight was described as a ‘freebie’ with a tour around the local area.  The instructor enjoying demonstrating the capabilities of the aircraft (loops and barrel rolls on request) with the pilot being limited to straight and level and a few gentle turns.  I later learned that there is no such thing as a freebie and that we were being assessed on the answer to every question and how much we had assimilated.  Every flight was followed by a thorough debrief.

Middle Wallop airfield is basically a massive grassy field.  The runway is marked using giant cones and there are set options so that it can be orientated more or less into wind.  There are 3 circuits: one direction, say anticlockwise, for fixed wing; the other direction, here clockwise, for basic rotary; and a third in the same direction, clockwise, but wider for advanced rotary.  There were more movements per day than at Heathrow.  As students we had set turnpoints on each circuit and many hours were spent watching a video of them and trying to memorise each one.

Then there were endless checks to be memorised.  Aircraft checks, pre-take off checks, downwind checks, pre-landing checks, HASELL (HOPE in BHPA speak).  Radio calls to be made, frequencies to be changed.  It was a very steep learning curve.

Over the course of 13 x daily 1-hour flights we were loaded with more and more.  The course was programmed to last 3 weeks but, as we all know the weather doesn’t always play ball, especially in February.  On some days we flew 2 sorties in order to catch up.  I had a change of instructor half way through and then another instructor for my final flight.

On the final afternoon we all waited nervously outside the CFI’s office, contemplating the fact that only 1 in 10 of us was likely to get through.  When my turn came it was all rather matter of fact and quickly over: a place on the next Army Pilot’s Course was mine!  I never saw any of the others on my grading course again, and quickly drove home to tell my wife that she needed to hand in her notice.  We needed to pack – we’d be moving house in the near future.

sections.title: Back in the Air - Part One
sections.content:
John Edmonds, HangParaglider Pilot reports

Where to start, well there must be a beginning, let's say choosing a University to study at, based on which one had an active Hang Gliding club!

This is 1976, no YouTube, Internet or email. Can you believe we wrote to one another, on paper! To get a reply took days but getting post was exciting. How I heard about Hang Gliding, I can’t remember, maybe the odd newspaper article or a piece on TV, what I saw and heard had me, so I chose Cardiff, it had Hang Gliding  on its sports page and I soon became the chairman of the club.

Understand, I was a married ‘mature student’, all of 26 but I had a house with a garage and a car so I got to look after the gliders. Nobody could go anywhere without my say so and I was keen, every opportunity to get on a hill, I was out.

Back in those days your success was determined by how long you flew for, maybe how high… XC hadn't been invented, a vario was a great big box with a flask in it and if air went out you were going up, an altimeter was a luxury! Definitely no radio or GPS, all line of sight.

Anyway, things moved on, we went, after a conversion course from seated, a seat like you find on a child's swing to a knee hanger, we called it prone. Literally, loops around the knees lying on your tummy on the swing seat with a couple of shoulder straps…..state of the art!

There’s a lot more, ask Trevor!

Baildon before the trees, maybe 1985…

Top landing at Baildon!

Fast forward to 1997, Joseph is 16, he got a Discovery 160 for Christmas (a Hang Glider) and lessons followed as weather permitted. I’d booked a holiday to Chamonix for the summer and the training hadn’t gone according to plan, Joseph got his CP doing a top to bottom the day before we were due to travel!

Dennis Trott was good to us and gave good instruction on the techniques of Alpine flying and Joseph was having a good time. As a family holiday we wanted to make it interesting and asked to visit Lake Annecy and Dennis obliged so off we went taking our gear on the car. On Joseph’s wall there is a picture I took of a ramp launch of him, have you ever done one? It’s a 20’ long wooden slope to nowhere, you go or you crash, pick up the glider balance and run like mad…

The thing I remember most is a radio message…’I’m at 7’000 ft, how do I get down?’

There were trips to Spain and Lanzarote with other stories, another time maybe but there were things happening in foot launch aviation, forget the aluminium, loose the tight preformed sail, get some material and lots of string and fly, paragliding was here. Along with pink neon shell suits…

Interested in this new idea we had a day with an independent spirit in the Peak district and insteads of taking days, even weeks, to free fly we had top-to-bottoms within a few hours.

This must have sparked something in Joseph, that and the fact that a successful flight for him was finishing the day with 2 straight uprights! So off he went and ploughed his furrow. Now he is able to hold his head up as an accomplished pilot, a member of the 100k club…ooohhh!

Me, well, getting old is no fun, still the suck of the thermal hauls you in and after deciding hang gliders are too heavy  I have tried my hand with these newfangled para things.

It’s taken ages to get from spending the cash on a Geo 3 and Verso to get to use it.

You know the story, weather, moving house, growing potatoes…oh yes, I always wanted to live off the land and invested in a house with land. It ends up that each potato I grow costs me about £1, not sound economy, enjoyable but not cost effective and the clock is ticking.

Trying to fit in training with tending onions, fixing roofs and all the other stuff at the new home has been tight. Don’t think I haven’t tried…. a week in Spain where it rained for all but ½ a day, time with Pete Morris when I hurt my ankle pushing a car which was stuck. Even a week in Oludeniz where I passed APPI 2 and flew from Babadag mountain. Any ground handling on Baildon moor had to be squeezed in.

So this year, this is the big one, like the ramp launch, do or don’t do!

2 Weeks in Bulgaria with SkyNomad, oh yes! EP and CP, what do you know, I can fly one of these paraglider things. That was until I got back and Joseph said I needed training on the ground, ‘You have to understand the principles and be at one with your wing, let it talk to you’ and I didn’t listen very well, I am a good student and it will come…..soon I hope!

Here another student lands, there is a ‘Gypsy’ village nearby and the boys run to ask for money, no chance! Take off at Dobrostan.

The bus with all the wood be pilots…

Yours truly togged up and raring to go from the Dobrostan take off…

Often when we were waiting for a time a take off the bus doubled as a white board, lessons outside, particularly good when talking about the weather!

This is Sopot, it takes a southerly and this day it was off to the east so it was, launch then turn right to go towards the landing field, we all managed

To end this story I’ll tell you I’m going back to Bulgaria to continue my training….how to thermal. Nowadays it’s not just the flying, there is all the weather forecasting, instrument preparation, hydration and calorific balance, much more complicated than when I was a lad!

If you enjoyed this ramble, make sure you check out next month when I’ll talk about my next trip to Bulgaria, where I went from top to bottom, right up to 3000+m cloudbase and 40+km XC flight!

sections.title: Coaching
sections.content:
Pete Logan, Chief Coach reports

This is probably more of a safety thing, but Clive’s got two articles this month so…

I was on an XC in August and found myself stuck with a few others trying to get a decent climb north out of Wensleydale.  

The ground around Barden Fell was heavily crossed by tracks and was right next to Catterick Barracks. It looked a bit military and the gaggle had a quick chat about it on the radio.

This definitely wasn’t in either the Feldom or Bellerby Danger Areas though, we’d decided to fly round to the south of those to take advantage of the better clouds. It did appear in my XCTrack maps though:

After a bit of ferreting about when I’d got back home it turns out it was Catterick Training Area. There are quite a few other places that are similarly marked - Menwith Hill (the Golf Balls) is one too. Other flight apps are going to vary. FlySkyHigh on Apple does not display them so it’s worth checking yours to make sure it does.

North England - public access to military areas - GOV.UK 

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=S0DmZtSgjq4 

They have roads and public footpaths through them and are commonly accessed by the public so we can land in them?…. Hmmm. They can be active at any time and, if so, are marked with red flags. We’d have little chance of seeing those flags from height though, so we’re entering the area not certain that it is inactive. And XCTrack marks prison grounds and GCHQ sites in the same colour! My conclusion is, if you go over any of them, know that you can land clear.

Qudos to XCTrack for showing us something we’ve not had on flight apps before.

sections.title: Membership
sections.content:
Rahul Basu reports

Welcome to our new members since April. The Dales is truly an inspiring place to fly, as you will find out. When on the hill please introduce yourself to the other members (If there aren't any then it's probably not flyable), who will gladly help out anyway they can.

Gustav Delius

I started paragliding in 1991 and obtained my full pilot licence in Switzerland in 1995. Most of my flying was in the Alps, especially ridge soaring on the Saleve in Geneva, where I lived for a while. When I moved to York in 1999 I stopped flying and only recently restarted, back at Club Pilot level. Besides the DHPC I’ve joined the Cayley Paragliding Club and the North Yorks Sailwing Club. I fly a Nova Ion 4. I am eager to get to know your sites and to learn from your coaches.

I hope to meet you soon.

Ray McGarry: Paraglider CP rating.

MICHAL SIKORA: Paraglider CP rating and fly’s a Skywalk chili5,  April

Steve Scholefield: Paraglider CP and fly’s a Nova Ion

Adrian Robertson: Paraglider P rating

Katharine Kirkbride:  Paraglider  CP  rating and fly’s a Flow future ENA  May

Matthew Vaughan:Paraglider  EP rating and fly’s a  Nova Ion 7

Andy Parker: Paraglider  CP rating

Jon Adamson: Paraglider CP rating

Qureshi Ahmed:  Paraglider CP rating

Jordan Whittaker: Paraglider CP rating.

sections.title: First Aid Course update
sections.content:
Rob Urselmann reports

Following a fantastic uptake, the first aid course on 2 November at Bradley Village hall is now fully booked and paid up with the maximum of 12 participants. However, it’s only just so you can still get your name down on the reserve list and likely be first in the queue. If we get enough, we might even consider a second course!   If interested, please contact me at firstaid@dhpc.org.uk 

BTW: Not doing kids, I was not aware the weekend is in school mid-term. Apologies if that was the reason you can’t take part.

subsections.title: Saturday 12th of October 2024
The Knights Table, Stainforth, BD24 0DP
subsections.content:
(Just North of Settle)

https://www.the-knights-table.co.uk/

Dress code Smart Casual, 7:30pm for 8pm

The club is lucky in that we don’t pay site fees.  Instead, we buy the farmers a bottle of their favourite tipple at Christmas, and invite each couple to an annual dinner where they get a free meal and drinks.  Historically this has worked very well as the invitation cost us nothing whilst making us appear very generous and spreading goodwill throughout the local community.  We tread a fine line because if they all accepted the invitation, we’d soon be bankrupt (and wouldn’t be able to find a venue big enough!).  Fortunately, it’s normally only the same dozen or so farmers that attend.

This year we have separated the dinner from the Fly-In and X-Dales, as we felt that it rather detracted from the atmosphere around the campfire last year.  We’ve also moved our annual prize giving to a club night.  As a result we anticipate a reduction in the number of members attending, with hosting duties falling mostly to committee members who will get a free meal for providing their services.  That said, you’d be more than welcome to attend to support the committee.  The farmers are a friendly bunch and you may reap the benefits when you need a retrieve off some remote moorland, or the loan of a ladder to get your wing out of a tree.

Following last year’s excellent food and service, we go back to the Knights Table at Stainforth again. We meet in the bar around 7.30pm with the aim of sitting down for dinner at 8pm.  The dress code is Smart Casual because the farmers quite like to dress up, but please wear whatever makes you feel comfortable.  The Chairman will make a (hopefully) short speech thanking the farmers before they depart at 11pm.

If you have a campervan or touring caravan Knight Stainforth has pitches available.  Alternatively there is plenty of accommodation, a short taxi drive away, in Settle.

Booking

The cost of the 3 - course dinner is £26 per person.  You pay for your own drinks at the bar.  If you would like to attend please make payment by BACS to the DHPC Main Account; 08-92-99; 65335956; using your surname as a reference, and then drop Dave Bradwell an email by 13th September 2024 letting us know your preferred meal choices (use the numbers).

treasurer@dhpc.org.uk (Dave Bradwell)

Menu

Starters

1.        Roasted mushroom and potato soup topped with korma cream and served with warm homemade roll and butter.

2.        Chicken Yakitori - marinated chicken, skewered and grilled with a sweet chilli glaze, toasted sesame seeds and crispy seaweed.

3.        Popcorn prawns – crispy fried Greenland prawns with a sweet chilli dip.

Main Course

4.        Beef Stifado – slow cooked Maudsley beef with red wine, onions, tomato, and oregano topped with grilled halloumi and served with roast potatoes.

5.        Crispy belly Pork - Rubbed with garlic and herbs, slow roasted over bubble and squeak with a rich red wine gravy served with vegetables.

6.        Fresh salmon fillet - grilled with jasmine rice, stir fried green vegetables, a spicy teriyaki glaze and shrimp crackers.

7.        Roasted shallot, tenderstem broccoli and stilton tart, baked with a mature cheddar crumble, served with mixed salad leaves and chips.

 Desserts

8.        Apple and blackberry crumble tart with custard.

9.        Vanilla cheesecake topped with blueberry compote.

10.         Brandy snap basket filled with salted caramel ice cream.

sections.title: From the Archive
sections.content: Martin Baxter brings you 3 articles from 1987.

subsections.title: Or rather, getting the right paraglider wing appropriate for your flying needs and abilities.
subsections.content:
At the end of last year I bought a high B wing.  The intention was to be a step up from my U Turn Infinity 4 which is a Low B.  My reasons for changing wings was to have increased flying performance, benefit from improvements in technology and improve my flying skills through handling a more advanced wing.

However, what I discovered was that the difference in wings has been much more pronounced than I intended, and consequently I’ve changed wings again.  This wasn’t a light decision but necessary for my own safety.  Hence why I’m sharing this for other flyers.  

I tried the new wing for about 10 hours flying and several ground handling sessions.  Indeed after several times ground handling I thought I’d got to grips with it.  Although this proved not to be the case and after a final time struggling with it in a high wind launch, I figured the decision to change had been made for me.  What I’d also noticed was that there had been times when the wing wasn’t fully inflated on launch, the risers got tangled easily with the partially unsheathed lines and asymmetric collapses were more common.  I accept that this has to do with my competence and not the glider, and I’ve omitted citing the specific wing since this isn’t a review of its characteristics (indeed by all other accounts it’s a great wing for the right flyer), the issue rests with me.  For another pilot it’s probably ideal and they’ll have a lot of fun with it.  But I had to acknowledge that I was at risk and wasn’t having fun.

What I’ve changed to is the Skywalk Arak 2.  I like the build quality, riser setup and technology in Skywalk gliders with the Arak 2 being defined as a mid B.  The Arak 2 has an aspect ratio of 5.2 with the previous Arak having a ratio of 5.6.  Despite this, Skywalk state that the glide performance on this second generation has improved, yet it has more stability and passive safety.  I used the Arak 2 for the first time this week and immediately recognised the difference.  For me, it was easier to control when launching, felt more stable in the air on glide and when thermalling.  Plus, I had more confidence landing.  The bottom line is that whilst I can’t afford to spend money unnecessarily, it’s been worth it to have the right glider for my ability and to feel more in control.

The purpose of this article is that by sharing this experience, other pilots that may be considering changing gliders can consider the differences fully before committing to a new wing.  Before buying the High B I’d flown a Mentor 5 Light which is also a high B, although crucially I hadn’t tried the Mentor in a variety of conditions.  To reiterate, I’d underestimated how much more demanding moving to another class of wing would be for me. As a low airtime pilot, if asked, I’d caution others before getting more challenging wings.  In an ideal world an extensive trial would be best.  In any event, a move down to the mid B Arak 2 has (so far) worked out for me and I highly recommend it.

I’d also like to mention that Steve at The Sick and The Wrong couldn’t have been more helpful in making it easy to change with High B as a trade-in against the Arak 2.  Thanks Steve.

subsections.title: Safety in action
subsections.content:
This week witnessed an incident on Windbank.  The leadership and management of the situation by Jordan Whittaker (CP+15 and new to the site) was highly commendable and worth noting.  I hope he’s not embarrassed by me mentioning this but it’s worth noting here for others to learn from.  By all accounts, Jordan reacted quickly to secure the injured pilot’s wing, assisted them to a safer area, and then aided the pilot down the hill at his own insistence.  Jordan contacted emergency services and, when the helicopter arrived, he let others know on the radio.  

The fuller details of this will be within the incident report for any lessons learnt and I wish the injured pilot all the best with his recovery.  One key aspect though is the effect adrenaline may have on an injured person. Steve and I met the injured pilot on the way up the hill and were assured by him that he had a sprained ankle and was ‘mostly’ fine.  It transpired his injuries were more severe than this.  Discussing this aspect later in the day it is common for injured parties to downplay and even not be aware of the extent of their wounds due to adrenaline and shock. (As I can personally testify when breaking a bone in my hand and not getting it treated at the time).  Therefore, it’s worth keeping this aspect in mind to ensure that anyone in an accident gets their injuries fully assessed by professional medics - and as soon as possible.

Safe flying,

Clive.

Martin Baxter responds.  Thanks to Clive for highlighting this important lesson.  Pilot’s insisting that they are OK to walk down the hill following an accident is nothing new.  I’m reminded of another incident at Windbank in 2018.   Kerim, a very experienced pilot, was hosting his less experienced brother in law.  In Kerim’s words:

He tried to turn back into wind from the downwind leg, pulled and pulled a bit more. The brake travel on the Ion 5 is quite short for its class and just when I took a picture the glider went negative. He was about 60 feet high and never made any attempts to let the wing fly again until he hit the ground very hard. He was very lucky to miss the rocks and land on a soft patch.

He insisted that nothing was wrong with him (like everyone does) and I made a very stupid mistake to let him walk down the Hill and go for a Sunday Roast as if nothing had happened. Obviously he felt terrible after that and we rushed him to A&E in Keighley. When we got there (...) he was diagnosed with a broken rib, 3 broken spinous processes, a broken leg and a small amount of blood in his lung.

We were so lucky that it was nothing worse. I promise to go by the procedure next time and call mountain rescue and tell them that the victim suffered a fall from height and we can't assess the extent of his injuries. Why have I been so stupid?

One final point.  If you witness an incident, or are the subject (and able to comply) then please submit an Incident Report within 48 hours.  Even if there is no third party claim, our insurers get twitchy if they hear about it on social media, but don’t know the details.  They may even put money aside to meet a potential claim (for up to a year) which can have a detrimental effect on our renewal fees.  More importantly it keeps the BHPA off my back!

sections.title: PG Tips 🫖 (Paragliding tips)
sections.content:
Stephen Craven reports

I’ve been flying with a half wrap on the brakes for a few years now. The reason is mainly safety but also comfort too. The advantage of this grip is that you can get your hands out of the brakes more easily than the grip where the stiff part of the handle goes across the back of your hand. If you find your brakes a bit long with this, then you can take an extra full wrap and still be safe. Gloves usually have stiffened bottom edges so that you can avoid the string digging in.

https://www.youtube.com/shorts/MvRg4IkSxJo
